q: What is the core difference between git merge and git rebase?
a: git merge takes the changes from two branches and joins them with a new merge commit, preserving the actual history with both parent lineages. git rebase instead replays your branch's commits one by one on top of the target branch's tip, creating new commits with new SHAs and producing a linear history. Merge is non-destructive and keeps context; rebase rewrites history and should only be done on commits you have not yet shared.
fs: Merge preserves true topology with a merge commit; rebase replays commits as new SHAs for a linear history, so never rebase shared/pushed commits.
fj: Rebase and merge do the same thing, I just pick whichever and rebase is always cleaner so I rebase everything including the shared main branch.

code.label: merge vs rebase a feature branch

followups.q: If both merge and rebase produce the same final tree, why does the choice still matter?
followups.a: The resulting working tree is identical, but the recorded history differs. Merge keeps the true branching topology so you can see what was developed in parallel, while rebase fabricates a linear story that never actually happened. That history is what `git bisect`, `git blame`, and reverts traverse, so the topology you record shapes how easily you can debug later.

followups.q: How does a conflict during a rebase differ from a conflict during a merge?
followups.a: A merge resolves all conflicts at once in a single merge commit. A rebase replays each commit in turn, so the same logical conflict can resurface at multiple commits and you may resolve it repeatedly. You fix each, run `git rebase --continue`, and can bail with `git rebase --abort`; enabling `rerere` reuses earlier resolutions.

followups.q: When would you deliberately prefer a merge commit over a clean rebase?
followups.a: When the branch is already shared, or when you want an auditable record of when a feature integrated, such as a release or a long-lived integration branch. A `--no-ff` merge commit groups the feature's commits and gives a natural revert point. Rebasing here would either be unsafe or erase meaningful integration context.

q: When should you NOT rebase, and why is the golden rule important?
a: The golden rule of rebasing is: never rebase commits that exist outside your repository, i.e. anything you have already pushed and others may have based work on. Rebasing rewrites commit SHAs, so collaborators who pulled the old commits end up with divergent history and painful re-merges. Keep rebase for cleaning up your own local, unshared feature branch before opening a PR.
fs: Only rebase private, unpushed commits; rebasing shared history forces everyone onto rewritten SHAs and corrupts their local branches.
fj: I rebase main and force-push so my history looks tidy; if teammates have issues they can just re-clone.

followups.q: Concretely, what happens to a teammate's clone after you rebase and force-push a branch they already pulled?
followups.a: Their local branch still points at the old SHAs, which no longer exist on the remote. Their next `git pull` sees diverged history and either creates a confusing merge that reintroduces the old commits or, with `pull.rebase`, replays their local work onto the rewritten base. Either way the same changes appear under two SHAs, producing duplicate commits and conflict noise.

followups.q: Is it ever acceptable to rebase a branch that has been pushed?
followups.a: Yes, on a personal feature branch that only you work on, even after pushing, as long as you coordinate and use `git push --force-with-lease`. The hazard is shared branches with multiple contributors. The rule is really about who else depends on the commits, not whether they touched a remote.

followups.q: If someone force-pushed a rewrite over commits you depend on, how do you recover your old base?
followups.a: Use `git reflog` or your remote-tracking ref's reflog (`git reflog origin/feature`) to find the pre-rewrite SHA, since the old commits linger in your object store until `gc`. Anchor it with `git branch salvage <sha>`, then rebase or cherry-pick your work onto the new history. Acting before garbage collection runs is what makes recovery possible.

q: Compare trunk-based development with Git Flow as branching models.
a: Trunk-based development keeps everyone committing to a single main (trunk) with very short-lived feature branches merged daily, relying on feature flags and CI to keep main releasable. Git Flow uses long-lived develop and main branches plus dedicated feature, release, and hotfix branches with strict merge rules. Trunk-based optimizes for continuous delivery and fewer merge conflicts; Git Flow suits scheduled releases and versioned products but adds branch overhead.
fs: Trunk-based favors short-lived branches plus flags and CD; Git Flow's long-lived develop/release/hotfix branches suit versioned, scheduled releases.
fj: Branching models are all basically the same, you just make a branch and merge it whenever, the names do not matter.

followups.q: Why does trunk-based development depend so heavily on feature flags?
followups.a: Because incomplete work merges to a shared trunk daily, flags keep that unfinished code dark in production until it is ready. They decouple deploy from release, letting you ship continuously without long-lived branches. Without flags, frequent merging of partial features would either break main or force the long branches trunk-based is trying to avoid.

followups.q: What hidden cost does Git Flow's develop/release/hotfix structure add over time?
followups.a: Long-lived branches diverge, so merges between `develop`, `release`, and `main` accumulate conflicts and demand careful, error-prone back-merging (a hotfix on `main` must be merged back into `develop`). The ceremony slows integration and raises the chance a fix is lost. That merge-debt is exactly the friction trunk-based avoids by keeping branches short.

followups.q: What signals tell you a team should move from Git Flow to trunk-based?
followups.a: Painful release-branch merges, frequent hotfix-back-merge mistakes, slow integration, and a desire for continuous deployment all point toward trunk-based. The prerequisites are strong CI, fast tests, and feature-flag tooling. Without that safety net, dropping Git Flow's gates can let broken code reach a continuously deployed main.

q: How do you resolve a merge conflict in Git step by step?
a: When a conflict occurs, Git pauses the merge and marks the conflicting regions in the affected files with <<<<<<<, =======, and >>>>>>> markers showing your changes versus theirs. You edit each file to the desired final state, remove the markers, then git add the resolved files to stage them and run git commit (or git merge --continue) to finish. You can inspect conflicts with git status, and abort entirely with git merge --abort to return to the pre-merge state.
fs: Edit out the <<<< / ==== / >>>> markers to the intended state, git add to mark resolved, then commit; git merge --abort cleanly bails out.
fj: I just delete one whole side of the conflict markers without reading it, or I keep both versions including the marker lines and commit.

followups.q: What does a three-way conflict view with merge.conflictStyle=diff3 add over the default markers?
followups.a: `diff3` adds a `|||||||` section showing the common ancestor's version between the two sides. Seeing the original lets you tell what each side actually changed rather than guessing, which is invaluable when both sides edited the same region. It turns a blind pick into an informed merge of two intentional changes.

followups.q: How can a merge succeed with no conflict markers yet still be broken?
followups.a: Git merges textually, not semantically. If one branch renames a function and another adds a new call to the old name in a different file, both apply cleanly but the code no longer compiles, a so-called semantic conflict. This is why a green merge still needs CI and tests, not just a clean text resolution.

followups.q: Which command resolves a binary file conflict, since you cannot edit markers in it?
followups.a: You pick a whole side with `git checkout --ours <file>` or `--theirs <file>`, then `git add` it, because binary blobs cannot be line-merged. During a merge, `ours` is your current branch and `theirs` is the incoming one (the sense inverts under rebase). For real binary merging you need a configured custom merge driver.

q: What does git bisect do and how do you use it?
a: git bisect performs a binary search through your commit history to find the exact commit that introduced a bug. You run git bisect start, mark a known-bad commit with git bisect bad and a known-good one with git bisect good, and Git checks out the midpoint for you to test; you mark each checkout good or bad until it isolates the first bad commit. This turns a linear scan of N commits into about log2(N) tests, then you run git bisect reset to return to where you started.
fs: Bisect binary-searches between a good and bad commit in log2(N) steps to pinpoint the first bad commit, then reset returns HEAD to its original spot.
fj: I just check out random old commits one at a time until I find the bug, bisect is too complicated to bother with.

followups.q: What do you do when a commit Git checks out during bisect cannot even be tested?
followups.a: Mark it `git bisect skip`, and Git picks a nearby commit instead of forcing a good/bad verdict on a broken build. If too many around it are unbuildable the result may be reported as a range rather than one commit. Skip keeps the search honest by never mislabeling an untestable revision.

followups.q: Why does bisect assume monotonicity, and what breaks the search if that assumption is false?
followups.a: Binary search assumes that once the bug appears it stays present, so every commit after the first bad one is also bad. If the bug is intermittent or was fixed and reintroduced, a flaky test gives a wrong good/bad answer and bisect converges on the wrong commit. A deterministic reproduction is essential for trustworthy results.

followups.q: How do you narrow a bisect to only the commits that touched a specific area?
followups.a: Pass pathspecs: `git bisect start -- src/auth/` limits the search to commits affecting that path, skipping unrelated ones and reducing test count. This is powerful when you already suspect a subsystem. It works because bisect only needs to test commits that could plausibly have changed the behavior.

q: How can you fully automate git bisect to find a regression?
a: git bisect run lets you hand Git a script or command that it executes at each step; an exit code of 0 marks the commit good, any non-zero (except 125 for untestable, and codes above 127) marks it bad. Git then drives the entire binary search unattended and reports the first bad commit. This is ideal in CI or when you have a reliable test or reproduction that exits with a meaningful status code.
fs: git bisect run <cmd> automates the search using exit codes (0 good, non-zero bad, 125 skip), so a deterministic test isolates the regression hands-free.
fj: There is no way to automate bisect, you always have to sit there and manually mark every commit good or bad yourself.

followups.q: Why is exit code 125 special, and what happens if your script returns it for the wrong reason?
followups.a: 125 means the commit is untestable, so Git skips it like a manual `git bisect skip` rather than treating it as bad. If a build failure returns 1 instead of 125, that revision is wrongly marked bad and the search lands on the wrong commit. Scripts should distinguish build failures from test failures and exit 125 for the former.

followups.q: How do you stop a bisect-run script from accidentally returning exit code 128 or higher?
followups.a: Codes 128 and above abort the entire `bisect run` because Git reserves them, so a test tool that exits, say, 255 on failure would kill the session. Wrap the command so any failure normalizes to a 1-to-124 range, for example `cmd; test $? -eq 0 || exit 1`. This keeps a legitimate failure from being misread as a fatal abort.

followups.q: How would you keep the test environment consistent at every commit the script visits?
followups.a: Rebuild or reinstall dependencies inside the script itself, since checking out an old commit does not restore old build artifacts or lockfile state. A robust runner does a clean build, runs the repro, and returns 125 if the build fails. Pinning the toolchain and clearing caches prevents a stale environment from poisoning the verdict.

q: What is the difference between git revert and git reset?
a: git revert creates a brand-new commit that applies the inverse of a target commit's changes, so history is preserved and nothing is rewritten, which makes it safe on shared branches. git reset moves the branch pointer (and optionally the index/working tree) backward, discarding or unstaging commits and rewriting history. Use revert to undo published commits; use reset to undo local commits you have not shared.
fs: Revert adds an inverse commit and is safe on public branches; reset rewinds the branch pointer and rewrites history, so reserve it for unpushed work.
fj: Revert and reset are the same undo button, so I git reset --hard on main and force-push to remove a bad commit everyone already pulled.

followups.q: How do you revert a merge commit, and why does it need an extra flag?
followups.a: A merge has two parents, so Git cannot infer which lineage to undo; you specify the mainline with `git revert -m 1 <merge-sha>`, where 1 is usually the branch you merged into. This reverses the branch's changes while keeping the mainline. It is needed because the revert must know which parent represents the baseline to restore.

followups.q: If you revert a feature merge, what surprise do you hit when you try to merge that branch again later?
followups.a: Git thinks those commits are already integrated, so re-merging brings in nothing while the revert still cancels the original work. You must first revert the revert (or rebase the branch onto a fresh base) to reintroduce the changes. This catches teams off guard after rolling back a feature they later want to ship.

followups.q: Both revert and reset can produce a clean tree at an old state, so why prefer revert on a shared branch even though history grows?
followups.a: Revert never rewrites existing SHAs, so everyone's clones stay valid and pulls fast-forward cleanly. Reset rewrites history, which on a shared branch forces a force-push that diverges every collaborator's checkout. The extra inverse commit is a small price for not breaking everyone downstream.

q: Explain the three modes of git reset: --soft, --mixed, and --hard.
a: All three move HEAD (the branch pointer) to the target commit but differ in how far the change propagates. --soft moves only HEAD, leaving the staging index and working tree intact, so the undone changes stay staged. --mixed (the default) moves HEAD and resets the index but keeps the working tree, leaving changes unstaged. --hard moves HEAD and overwrites both the index and the working tree to match the target, permanently discarding uncommitted changes.
fs: --soft moves HEAD only (changes stay staged), --mixed also resets the index (changes unstaged), --hard also wipes the working tree, discarding edits.
fj: git reset --hard is just the normal undo, I use it all the time even with uncommitted changes I want to keep.

followups.q: Which reset mode would you use to squash the last three commits into one, and why?
followups.a: Use `git reset --soft HEAD~3`, which moves HEAD back three commits but leaves all their changes staged in the index. You then run a single `git commit` to create one combined commit. `--soft` is right because it preserves the work as staged content rather than discarding or unstaging it.

followups.q: Is data truly unrecoverable after git reset --hard, given the commits still exist somewhere?
followups.a: Committed work survives: the orphaned commits remain in the object store and `git reflog` plus `git branch <name> <sha>` recovers them until `gc` runs. What is genuinely lost is uncommitted working-tree changes, which were never objects and leave no reflog trace. So `--hard` is recoverable for commits but not for unsaved edits.

followups.q: How does git reset <commit> -- <path> behave differently from a plain reset?
followups.a: With a pathspec, reset does not move HEAD at all; it only copies that file's version from the target commit into the index, effectively unstaging or restaging that path. The `--soft`/`--hard` flags are not allowed in this form. It is a surgical index edit, not a branch-pointer move.

q: What does git cherry-pick do and when would you use it?
a: git cherry-pick applies the changes introduced by one or more specific commits onto your current branch, creating new commits with new SHAs that reproduce those diffs. It is useful for porting a single bug fix from one branch to another (for example backporting a hotfix to a release branch) without merging the entire source branch. Because it duplicates the change as a new commit, cherry-picking the same fix into multiple branches can create divergent SHAs and later merge noise.
fs: Cherry-pick replays specific commits' diffs as new SHAs onto the current branch, ideal for backporting a hotfix without merging the whole source branch.
fj: Cherry-pick moves a commit so it is no longer on the original branch, like cutting and pasting it between branches.

code.label: backport a fix to release branch

followups.q: If you cherry-pick a fix into release and the branches later merge, why might the same change appear twice?
followups.a: The cherry-pick created a new commit with a different SHA, so Git treats it as distinct from the original. At merge time, if the diffs do not overlap cleanly, the change can apply a second time or conflict. Git's patch-id heuristics and `rerere` help, but duplicate logical changes are the known cost of cherry-picking across branches that later converge.

followups.q: What does cherry-pick -x add to the commit message, and why does it help?
followups.a: `-x` appends a `(cherry picked from commit <sha>)` line, recording the source commit. That breadcrumb lets reviewers and future you trace a backport to its origin and spot duplicates during a later merge. It is standard practice on public release branches where provenance matters.

followups.q: When is cherry-picking a smell, and what should you do instead?
followups.a: Repeatedly cherry-picking many commits between long-lived branches signals you should merge or rebase the branch instead, since piecemeal picks fragment history and breed conflicts. Cherry-pick is best for one-off, isolated fixes like a hotfix. If you are picking a whole feature, integrate the branch properly.

q: How do you use interactive rebase to clean up a feature branch?
a: git rebase -i <base> opens an editor listing your commits with action keywords you can change: pick keeps a commit, reword edits its message, squash folds it into the previous commit and combines messages, fixup folds it in discarding its message, edit pauses to amend, and drop removes it. Reordering the lines reorders the commits. This lets you collapse messy WIP commits into a clean, reviewable history before opening a PR, but only do it on unshared commits.
fs: rebase -i lets you reorder, reword, squash, fixup, edit, or drop commits to craft a clean reviewable history, but only on commits you have not pushed.
fj: Interactive rebase just reorders commits, you cannot combine or delete them, and it is safe to run on main after pushing.

followups.q: What is the difference between squash and fixup in the rebase todo list?
followups.a: Both fold a commit into the one above it, but `squash` opens an editor to combine both commit messages, while `fixup` silently discards the squashed commit's message and keeps the target's. Use `fixup` for trivial corrections like typos and `squash` when the message has content worth merging. The choice is purely about whether the message matters.

followups.q: You used edit on a mid-history commit and amended it. Why do later commits get new SHAs too?
followups.a: A commit's SHA hashes its content plus its parent. Changing one commit alters its SHA, which changes the parent of the next, cascading new SHAs through every descendant. This is why even editing one old commit rewrites the whole branch tip and demands a `--force-with-lease` if it was pushed.

followups.q: If a rebase goes wrong halfway through, how do you get back to your original branch state?
followups.a: Run `git rebase --abort` to restore the branch to exactly where it was before the rebase started. If you already finished a bad rebase, `git reflog` shows the pre-rebase tip (often `HEAD@{n}` before the rebase entries) and `git reset --hard` to it recovers. The original commits persist until garbage collection.

q: What do git commit --fixup and rebase --autosquash do together?
a: git commit --fixup=<sha> creates a commit whose message is prefixed with 'fixup! <target subject>', marking it as belonging to that earlier commit. When you later run git rebase -i --autosquash, Git automatically reorders that fixup commit directly after its target and sets its action to fixup, so it folds in without manual editing. This gives a fast, reliable workflow for amending review feedback into the right original commit; squash! works the same but keeps the message for editing.
fs: commit --fixup tags a commit to a target SHA, and rebase -i --autosquash auto-reorders and folds it in, so review fixes land in the original commit.
fj: There is no special way to do this, you have to manually drag fixup commits around in the rebase editor every single time.

followups.q: How does --autosquash actually match a fixup commit to its target?
followups.a: It parses the `fixup! <subject>` prefix Git wrote and matches the trailing text against existing commit subjects to find the target. You can also pass a SHA prefix in the message for an unambiguous match. Because it is subject-based, two commits with identical subjects can confuse the matching.

followups.q: How do you avoid typing --autosquash on every rebase?
followups.a: Set `git config --global rebase.autosquash true`, after which any interactive rebase auto-arranges `fixup!` and `squash!` commits. You can still override per-invocation with `--no-autosquash`. This makes the fixup workflow the frictionless default.

followups.q: What is the difference between fixup! and squash! prefixes during autosquash?
followups.a: Both reorder under the target, but `fixup!` discards the follow-up commit's message while `squash!` opens an editor to combine the messages. Use `--fixup` for silent corrections and `--squash` when the addition has message content worth keeping. They mirror the `fixup` versus `squash` todo actions.

q: Walk through a healthy pull request review flow.
a: You branch off main, make focused commits, and open a PR describing the intent, linking the issue, and keeping the diff small and reviewable. Automated CI runs tests, linters, and security checks, while reviewers leave line comments; you address feedback with follow-up or fixup commits and re-request review. Once approvals and required status checks pass, the PR is merged (often via squash or rebase-merge to keep main linear) and the branch is deleted. Small, single-purpose PRs get reviewed faster and merge more safely.
fs: Small single-purpose PR, green CI as a required gate, line-comment feedback addressed via fixups, then squash/rebase-merge and delete the branch.
fj: I push a giant branch with weeks of work straight to main and ask for a thumbs-up after it is already merged.

followups.q: Why prefer adding fixup commits during review over force-pushing a rebased branch?
followups.a: Incremental commits let reviewers see exactly what changed since their last look, instead of re-reading a rewritten diff from scratch. Force-pushing a rebase can also break review threads and existing comments. You squash or autosquash the fixups only at the end, once approval lands, keeping the review traceable.

followups.q: What does a branch protection rule add that mere reviewer discipline does not?
followups.a: Protection rules enforce policy at the server: required passing checks, a minimum number of approvals, dismissal of stale reviews, and a block on direct pushes to `main`. Unlike client-side hooks or etiquette, they cannot be bypassed with `--no-verify`. They turn the healthy flow from a convention into a guarantee.

followups.q: Why does breaking a large change into several small PRs usually merge faster overall?
followups.a: Reviewers give small, single-purpose diffs deeper, quicker attention, while huge PRs sit because reviewing them is daunting and risky. Small PRs also conflict less and fail CI on a tighter blast radius. The serialized merges cost some coordination but net out faster and safer than one giant review.

q: What is git reflog and why is it a safety net?
a: git reflog records every change to where HEAD and branch tips have pointed locally, including movements that git log no longer shows such as resets, rebases, amends, and checkouts. Each entry has a reference like HEAD@{2} you can inspect or reset to, letting you recover commits that seem lost after a destructive operation. It is local-only and entries eventually expire (reachable ones after about 90 days, unreachable after 30) before garbage collection can remove the orphaned commits.
fs: Reflog logs every local HEAD/branch-tip move, so even after a bad reset or rebase you can find the old SHA and recover it before gc reclaims it.
fj: Once you run git reset --hard the old commits are gone forever, there is no way to get them back.

followups.q: Why can the reflog not help you on a freshly cloned repo or a teammate's machine?
followups.a: The reflog is strictly local and is never pushed or fetched; it records movements that happened in that specific working copy. A fresh clone starts with an empty reflog, so it cannot recover commits that only ever existed in someone else's checkout. Recovery via reflog must happen on the machine where the operation occurred.

followups.q: What is the difference between HEAD@{2} and master@{2}?
followups.a: `HEAD@{2}` walks HEAD's own reflog, two moves ago regardless of branch, while `master@{2}` walks the per-branch reflog for `master` specifically. Branch reflogs only log changes to that branch's tip, so the two can diverge. Choosing the right ref matters when you switched branches between the operations you are tracing.

followups.q: An orphaned commit you wanted is gone even though it was only a week old. What likely happened?
followups.a: Unreachable reflog entries expire after about 30 days by default (`gc.reflogExpireUnreachable`), but an explicit `git gc --prune=now` or an aggressive prune setting can drop them immediately. Pruning a commit that nothing references removes it for good. To be safe, anchor recovered work with a branch before running any `gc`.

q: You ran git reset --hard and lost commits. How do you recover them?
a: Run git reflog to find the SHA the branch pointed at before the reset (the unreachable commit is still in the object store). The safest recovery is to anchor it with a new branch using git branch recovered <sha> rather than overwriting your current branch, which prevents garbage collection and gives you a stable place to inspect. You can also use git cherry-pick or git merge from there, and you should avoid running git gc until the work is secured.
fs: Use reflog to find the pre-reset SHA, then anchor it with git branch <name> <sha> so gc cannot reclaim it; avoid git gc until secured.
fj: Lost commits from a hard reset are unrecoverable, so I just rewrite all the work from scratch.

followups.q: If the reset also blew away staged-but-uncommitted changes, can reflog recover those?
followups.a: No. The reflog only tracks commits, and uncommitted changes were never committed. However, content you had `git add`ed becomes a dangling blob in the object store, recoverable via `git fsck --lost-found` and reconstructed by hand. Truly never-staged edits leave no trace at all.

followups.q: Why anchor the lost SHA with a branch instead of just git reset --hard back to it?
followups.a: Resetting moves your current branch and could overwrite other work you have since done, and a typo in the SHA loses your place. Creating a branch is non-destructive: it makes the commit reachable so `gc` spares it, then you inspect and integrate deliberately. It separates recovery from the decision of what to keep.

followups.q: How does git fsck --lost-found complement the reflog for recovery?
followups.a: `git fsck --lost-found` scans the object store for dangling commits and blobs that no ref or reflog points to, surfacing work the reflog may have already expired or never logged. It is the deeper net when reflog comes up empty. The trade-off is you get raw SHAs with no context, so you must inspect each candidate.

q: What is a detached HEAD state and how do you safely keep work made in it?
a: HEAD is detached when it points directly at a commit SHA (or tag) instead of a branch reference, which happens when you git checkout a specific commit. You can look around and even commit, but those commits belong to no branch, so switching away leaves them unreachable and they can eventually be garbage collected. To keep work done in detached HEAD, create a branch at the current commit with git switch -c <name> (or git branch <name>) before checking out anything else.
fs: Detached HEAD points at a raw SHA, not a branch; commits made there are unreferenced, so git switch -c <branch> before leaving to save them.
fj: Detached HEAD is just a warning you can ignore; I commit there and switch branches freely, the commits are always safe.

code.label: save detached-HEAD commits
lines: # now the commit lives on a branch

followups.q: Name a routine workflow where detached HEAD is intentional and harmless.
followups.a: CI checkouts and bisect run in detached HEAD by design, because they only need to test a specific commit, not advance a branch. Inspecting an old tag or a historical SHA read-only is also fine. It only becomes risky when you create commits there and then leave without anchoring them.

followups.q: You already switched away and your detached commits are gone from git log. How do you get them back?
followups.a: Run `git reflog`; HEAD's reflog still records the detached commits as `HEAD@{n}` entries. Find the SHA and anchor it with `git branch saved <sha>`. They remain in the object store until garbage collection, so recovery is reliable if you act reasonably soon.

followups.q: Why does Git allow committing in detached HEAD at all instead of just refusing?
followups.a: Because detaching is a legitimate, powerful state: bisect, rebases, and experimental spikes all need to build commits not yet tied to any branch. Forbidding commits would cripple those workflows. Git's contract is to let you work freely and warn you, leaving the responsibility to anchor the result with a branch.

q: How does a fast-forward merge differ from a three-way merge?
a: A fast-forward merge happens when the target branch has no new commits since the feature branched, so Git simply moves the branch pointer forward to the feature's tip without creating a merge commit, leaving a perfectly linear history. A three-way (true) merge happens when both branches have diverged; Git uses the two tips and their common ancestor to compute a new merge commit with two parents. You can force a merge commit even when fast-forward is possible with git merge --no-ff to preserve the feature branch's grouping.
fs: Fast-forward just advances the pointer when history hasn't diverged; a diverged history needs a three-way merge commit, and --no-ff forces one to record the branch.
fj: Every merge always makes a merge commit, there is no such thing as fast-forward.

followups.q: Why do some teams enforce --no-ff for every feature merge despite the extra commit?
followups.a: The merge commit groups a feature's commits and records when it integrated, giving a single, clean point to revert the whole feature later. A fast-forward scatters those commits inline with no boundary marker. The trade-off is a less linear graph, accepted for the revertability and traceability it buys.

followups.q: What is the third input to a three-way merge, and why is it essential?
followups.a: The merge base, the common ancestor of the two tips, is the third input. Comparing each side against the base lets Git tell what each branch actually changed rather than treating every difference as a conflict. Without the base it could not distinguish an intentional edit from the original content.

followups.q: How can you require fast-forward and reject merges that would create a merge commit?
followups.a: Use `git merge --ff-only`, which succeeds only if the merge can fast-forward and aborts otherwise, or set `merge.ff = only`. This enforces a strictly linear history, common in trunk-based teams. The cost is you must rebase your branch onto the latest tip before merging.

q: What is the difference between git fetch and git pull?
a: git fetch downloads new commits, branches, and tags from the remote into your remote-tracking refs (like origin/main) without touching your working branch, so you can inspect changes before integrating. git pull is essentially git fetch followed by an integration step, by default a merge (or a rebase with --rebase or pull.rebase set), which updates your current branch immediately. Fetching first is safer because it lets you review incoming changes and choose how to integrate.
fs: Fetch updates remote-tracking refs only; pull = fetch + merge/rebase into the current branch, so fetch-then-review avoids surprise integrations.
fj: Fetch and pull are the same; I always pull because it is faster and I never need to look before merging.

followups.q: What does git pull --rebase change about how your local commits integrate, and why might a team mandate it?
followups.a: Instead of creating a merge commit, it replays your unpushed local commits on top of the fetched remote tip, keeping history linear. Teams mandate it to avoid a clutter of tiny 'Merge branch main' commits from routine pulls. The caveat is it rewrites your local commits, so only your own unpushed work should be in flight.

followups.q: Why do remote-tracking refs like origin/main sometimes go stale, and how do you clean them?
followups.a: When a branch is deleted on the remote, your `origin/<branch>` ref lingers until you prune it. Run `git fetch --prune` (or set `fetch.prune = true`) to drop refs whose upstream is gone. Without pruning, deleted branches keep appearing in `git branch -r`, misleading you about what still exists.

followups.q: After git fetch, which command shows what you would pull in versus what you would push out?
followups.a: `git log main..origin/main` shows incoming commits you do not yet have, and `git log origin/main..main` shows your local commits the remote lacks. The two-dot syntax is range exclusion. This pre-merge review is exactly the safety that fetching first, rather than blindly pulling, gives you.

q: What is the difference between git stash and a commit, and how do you use stash?
a: git stash takes your uncommitted staged and unstaged changes, saves them on a separate stack, and reverts your working tree to a clean HEAD, letting you switch context without making a commit. You restore them later with git stash pop (apply and drop) or git stash apply (apply and keep), and you can hold multiple stashes addressed as stash@{0}, stash@{1}. Unlike a commit, a stash is local, unnamed by default, and not part of branch history, so it is meant for short-lived work-in-progress, not durable storage.
fs: Stash shelves WIP onto a local stack and cleans the tree for context-switching; pop/apply restores it, but it is ephemeral, not a substitute for commits.
fj: Stash is a permanent backup of my work, so I leave dozens of stashes around for weeks instead of committing.

followups.q: By default git stash leaves new untracked files behind. How do you include them?
followups.a: Add `-u` (`--include-untracked`) to stash new files too, or `-a` (`--all`) to also include ignored files. The default skips untracked paths because they are not yet part of the tracked state. Forgetting `-u` is a common reason a 'clean' stash still leaves a half-finished new file in the tree.

followups.q: Why can git stash pop fail and leave you in a messy state, and what do you do then?
followups.a: If the stashed changes conflict with the current working tree, `pop` applies what it can, raises conflict markers, and crucially does not drop the stash. You resolve the conflicts, `git add`, then manually `git stash drop`. `apply` is safer for risky restores since it never deletes the entry.

followups.q: How would you turn a stash into a proper branch, and when is that better than popping?
followups.a: Use `git stash branch <name>`, which creates a branch from the commit the stash was made on, applies the stash, and drops it. It is ideal when the underlying code has moved on and a plain `pop` would conflict, since it restores onto the original base. This converts ephemeral WIP into real, committable history.

q: How does git rebase --onto let you transplant a range of commits?
a: git rebase --onto <newbase> <upstream> <branch> replays the commits in <branch> that are not in <upstream> onto <newbase>, effectively moving a slice of history to a new parent. It is the precise tool for situations like a feature branched off the wrong base, or extracting commits that were built on a branch you no longer want them attached to. The three arguments separate where to graft (newbase), the old cutoff point (upstream), and the branch whose commits move.
fs: rebase --onto <newbase> <upstream> <branch> transplants only the commits after <upstream> onto a new base, fixing a branch that started from the wrong parent.
fj: Rebase can only put a branch on top of another branch's tip, you cannot move just part of the history.

code.label: move feature off topic onto main

followups.q: In git rebase --onto A B C, what role does each of the three arguments play?
followups.a: `A` (newbase) is where the commits will be replanted, `B` (upstream) marks the old parent so commits after `B` up to `C` are the slice that moves, and `C` (branch) is the branch whose tip defines the end of the range. Git computes `B..C` and replays it onto `A`. Misreading `B` as the new base is the classic mistake.

followups.q: How would you use --onto to drop a few bad commits from the middle of a branch?
followups.a: Point upstream at the last commit you want to keep and replay the good tail onto it: `git rebase --onto <keep-sha> <last-bad-sha>`. Everything between those two SHAs is excluded from the replay, surgically removing the bad commits. It is `--onto` used to cut a hole, not just to move a base.

followups.q: What danger does --onto carry that a plain git rebase does not?
followups.a: Because you specify the upstream cutoff manually, an off-by-one in the SHA can silently drop or duplicate commits without any conflict to warn you. A plain rebase infers the merge base for you. So `--onto` demands you verify the result with `git log` and is safest run only on unshared branches you can redo.

q: Why is force-pushing dangerous and what makes --force-with-lease safer?
a: git push --force overwrites the remote branch with your local history unconditionally, so if a teammate pushed in the meantime, their commits are silently discarded. git push --force-with-lease only overwrites if the remote tip still matches the value you last fetched, meaning your local view of the remote is current; if someone else pushed, the push is rejected instead of clobbering their work. Use --force-with-lease whenever you must rewrite a branch you previously pushed, such as after an interactive rebase on your own feature branch.
fs: --force blindly replaces the remote and can drop teammates' commits; --force-with-lease aborts unless the remote tip is what you last saw, preventing silent loss.
fj: Force-push is fine, I always use --force on every branch including main because it just makes my push go through.

followups.q: How can a background git fetch silently defeat the protection of --force-with-lease?
followups.a: The lease compares against your local remote-tracking ref, not the live remote. If an editor or `fetch.prune`/autofetch updated `origin/branch` to include the teammate's commit, the lease now matches and the force succeeds, overwriting their work anyway. The fix is `--force-with-lease=<ref>:<sha>` pinning the exact SHA you expect.

followups.q: Even a clean force-push that drops no commits can disrupt collaborators. Why?
followups.a: Rewriting a branch they have checked out means their next pull diverges, forcing a merge or rebase of their local work onto new SHAs and breaking any open PR review threads. The commits are not lost, but everyone downstream pays a reconciliation cost. That is why you rewrite only branches you alone own.

followups.q: What server-side control prevents force-pushes regardless of which flag a developer uses?
followups.a: Branch protection rules on the remote can forbid force-pushes to `main` entirely, so neither `--force` nor `--force-with-lease` is accepted. This enforces an append-only shared history independent of client discipline. Developers then rely on `revert` rather than rewriting public branches.

q: What is the difference between git merge --squash and a regular merge?
a: A regular merge integrates the source branch and records a merge commit (or fast-forwards), preserving each individual commit and the branch topology. git merge --squash instead stages the combined result of all the source branch's changes as one set of working-tree changes without creating a commit or recording the merge parent, so you then make a single new commit. Squash merging gives main a clean one-commit-per-feature history but loses the granular commits and the link back to the branch, which can complicate future merges from that branch.
fs: Regular merge keeps every commit and the merge parentage; --squash collapses the branch into one staged change with no merge link, trading granularity for a tidy main.
fj: merge --squash is the same as a normal merge, it just automatically commits everything for you with both parents recorded.

code.label: squash a feature into one commit

followups.q: Because a squash merge records no merge parent, what happens if you later merge that same branch again?
followups.a: Git does not know the branch's commits are already integrated, since the squash commit shares no ancestry link with them. Re-merging replays every original commit, producing duplicate changes and likely conflicts. The fix is to delete the branch after a squash merge, or rebase it fresh before reusing it.

followups.q: What is the trade-off squash merging makes against tools like git bisect and git blame?
followups.a: Collapsing many commits into one yields a tidy main but coarsens debugging: bisect can only narrow to the whole feature, and blame attributes every changed line to a single squash commit. You lose the granular author and intent trail. Teams accept this for a clean history when individual WIP commits add little value.

followups.q: How does squash merge differ from squashing with interactive rebase before merging?
followups.a: `merge --squash` flattens the entire branch into one staged change at merge time with no merge link. Interactive rebase lets you selectively combine some commits while keeping others, preserving a curated multi-commit history that then merges normally. Rebase gives finer control; `--squash` is the blunt one-commit-per-feature instrument.

q: What is the difference between a lightweight tag and an annotated tag?
a: A lightweight tag is just a named pointer to a commit, stored as a simple ref with no extra data, much like a branch that never moves. An annotated tag is a full Git object that stores the tagger's name, email, and date, a message, and can be GPG-signed and verified. Use annotated tags for releases and milestones because they carry provenance and are what describe and most tooling expect; reserve lightweight tags for throwaway local markers.
fs: Lightweight tags are bare refs to a commit; annotated tags are real objects with tagger, date, message, and signing, so use annotated for any release.
fj: Tags are all the same; I always run git tag v1.0 and assume it records who tagged it and when.

followups.q: Why does git describe behave differently depending on which kind of tag you used?
followups.a: `git describe` by default considers only annotated tags, producing strings like `v1.2-14-gabc123` relative to the nearest one. A lightweight tag is invisible to it unless you pass `--tags`. This is a concrete reason release tooling expects annotated tags for version derivation.

followups.q: Why must tags be pushed explicitly, and what surprises teams that forget?
followups.a: `git push` does not transfer tags by default, so a release tag created locally never reaches the remote until you `git push origin <tag>` or `--tags`. Teams are caught out when CI keyed on a tag never triggers, or when collaborators cannot see the release marker. Tags are independent refs, not carried along with branch pushes.

followups.q: You pushed v1.0 to the wrong commit. How do you correctly move it, and why is that risky?
followups.a: You retag with `git tag -f v1.0 <correct-sha>` and `git push --force origin v1.0`. The risk is that anyone who already fetched the old tag keeps the stale value, since Git assumes tags are immutable and will not auto-update them. Moving a published tag breaks that contract, so it is strongly discouraged after release.

q: How does .gitignore work, and why does it not stop tracking a file you already committed?
a: .gitignore tells Git which untracked paths to leave out of status and staging, matched by glob patterns evaluated per directory. It only affects untracked files: once a file is tracked, adding it to .gitignore has no effect because Git keeps managing what it already knows about. To stop tracking a committed file you run git rm --cached to remove it from the index while keeping it on disk, then commit; the ignore rule then applies going forward.
fs: .gitignore only filters untracked paths, so already-committed files keep being tracked until you git rm --cached them and commit the removal.
fj: I added the file to .gitignore, so the secret I already committed will stop showing up and is no longer in the repo.

followups.q: You committed a secret, then ran git rm --cached and added it to .gitignore. Why is the secret still not safe?
followups.a: The secret remains in every historical commit, so anyone can check out the old version or browse history. You must purge it from history with `git filter-repo` (or BFG) and force-push, then rotate the credential since it must be assumed leaked. Untracking only stops future exposure, not past.

followups.q: How do you force-add a single file that an ignore rule excludes?
followups.a: Use `git add -f <file>` to override the ignore for that path, or add a negation pattern like `!important.log` in `.gitignore`. Negation only works if the file's parent directory is not itself ignored, since Git will not descend into an ignored directory. This handles the occasional exception to a broad ignore rule.

followups.q: Where besides .gitignore can ignore rules live, and how do they differ in scope?
followups.a: `.gitignore` is committed and shared with the repo; `.git/info/exclude` is local and unversioned for personal ignores; and `core.excludesFile` sets a global ignore across all your repos. Per-directory `.gitignore` files also nest. Use the committed file for project rules and the local ones for editor or OS cruft you do not want to impose on the team.

q: Compare GitHub Flow with Git Flow as branching strategies.
a: GitHub Flow is a lightweight model: main is always deployable, every change is a short-lived branch that opens a PR, and once reviewed and green it merges and deploys, often continuously. Git Flow is heavier, with long-lived main and develop branches plus dedicated feature, release, and hotfix branches and strict promotion rules. GitHub Flow suits web apps with continuous delivery and a single production line; Git Flow fits versioned software with parallel releases and scheduled cuts, at the cost of more branch management.
fs: GitHub Flow = main always deployable + short PR branches + continuous deploy; Git Flow adds develop/release/hotfix for versioned, scheduled releases.
fj: GitHub Flow and Git Flow are the same thing; both need a permanent develop branch and timed release branches.

followups.q: GitHub Flow has no release branch, so how do you support an old version in production while shipping new work?
followups.a: You cut a support branch from the relevant tag and cherry-pick or backport fixes onto it, since main always races ahead. This is exactly the gap Git Flow's release branches fill natively. GitHub Flow assumes a single production line, so multi-version support is bolted on rather than built in.

followups.q: Why does GitHub Flow lean so hard on feature flags and continuous deployment?
followups.a: Because every merge to main can deploy immediately, unfinished work must hide behind flags to keep production stable. Flags decouple merging from releasing, enabling the model's tight loop. Without robust CI and flagging, the absence of a staging `develop` branch would let half-baked changes reach users.

followups.q: What is GitLab Flow, and what gap between these two does it address?
followups.a: GitLab Flow keeps GitHub Flow's simplicity but adds environment branches (e.g. `staging`, `production`) or release branches that changes flow through via merges. It gives a controlled promotion path without Git Flow's full `develop`/`release`/`hotfix` ceremony. It is a middle ground for teams needing staged deploys but not parallel versioned releases.

q: What is the staging area (index) and how does git add -p use it?
a: The index, or staging area, is an intermediate snapshot between your working tree and the next commit: git add copies a file's current content into it, and git commit records exactly what the index holds, not what is on disk at commit time. This three-way separation lets you craft a commit that is a subset of your changes. git add -p walks each diff hunk interactively so you can stage only the parts that belong together, keeping unrelated edits out and producing focused, reviewable commits.
fs: The index is the staged snapshot that a commit captures; git add -p stages individual hunks so one messy working tree becomes several focused commits.
fj: Commit always captures whatever is on disk right now, so staging is pointless and I just git add . then commit everything together.

followups.q: If you stage a file with git add and then edit it again before committing, what gets committed?
followups.a: Only the version captured in the index at `add` time; the later edits stay unstaged and are excluded. This is the index's whole point, it snapshots content, not a live file reference. You would need a second `git add` to include the new edits, which surprises people who assume commit reads disk.

followups.q: What does the s (split) option in git add -p do, and when is it not available?
followups.a: `s` divides a hunk into smaller hunks so you can stage part of a contiguous diff block. It only works when the changes are separated by an unchanged context line; tightly adjacent edits cannot be split. For those you fall back to `e` to edit the hunk patch by hand.

followups.q: How do git stash --keep-index and the index interact during testing?
followups.a: `git stash --keep-index` shelves everything but leaves the staged changes in the working tree, so you can run tests against exactly what you are about to commit, not unrelated WIP. It leverages the index as the definition of the prospective commit. This verifies each focused commit independently before it lands.

q: What problem does git worktree solve compared to switching branches or cloning?
a: git worktree lets one repository have multiple working directories checked out at once, each on a different branch but sharing the same object store. Instead of stashing and switching branches (which churns your working tree and rebuilds) or making a second full clone (which duplicates history and drifts), you add a linked worktree to review a PR, run a long build, or hotfix while your main checkout stays untouched. You remove it with git worktree remove when done, and a branch can only be checked out in one worktree at a time.
fs: Worktrees give multiple checkouts from one repo sharing objects, so you hotfix or review a branch without stashing, switching, or a second clone.
fj: To work on two branches at once you must clone the repo twice; there is no way to have two branches checked out from one repo.

code.label: check out a second branch in parallel

followups.q: Why does Git refuse to check out the same branch in two worktrees at once?
followups.a: Both worktrees would share one branch ref, so a commit in one would silently move the other's HEAD out from under it, risking confusion and lost work. Git enforces one-checkout-per-branch to keep each worktree's HEAD coherent. You can instead create a new branch or use a detached checkout of the same commit.

followups.q: What does sharing one object store across worktrees gain you over a second full clone?
followups.a: No duplicated history or disk bloat, and fetches in one worktree immediately make objects available to all of them. A second clone drifts: its remotes, refs, and fetched commits are independent and must be synced separately. Worktrees keep a single source of truth while giving multiple working directories.

followups.q: What is a subtle risk of running builds in parallel worktrees of the same repo?
followups.a: They share `.git` but have separate working trees, so build tools writing to shared absolute paths or caches can collide, and submodule state can be inconsistent across worktrees. Hooks and config are shared too. You isolate per-worktree build output directories to avoid clobbering, which a fully separate clone would sidestep at the cost of duplication.

q: How do git blame and git log -L help you investigate when and why a line changed?
a: git blame annotates each line of a file with the commit, author, and date that last touched it, so you can find which change introduced a line and jump to its commit message and PR for context. For deeper history of a specific region you use git log -L to follow the full revision history of a line range or function across renames, and git log -S (the pickaxe) to find commits that added or removed a given string. Together they answer who, when, and why far faster than scrolling diffs.
fs: blame shows the last commit per line; log -L follows a line range's whole history and log -S finds where a string was added or removed.
fj: blame just shows the current author of the file and cannot tell you which commit changed a specific line or why.

followups.q: A reformatting commit makes blame attribute every line to it, hiding the real authors. How do you see past it?
followups.a: Use `git blame --ignore-rev <sha>` for a single noisy commit, or list such commits in a `.git-blame-ignore-revs` file referenced via `blame.ignoreRevsFile`. Git then skips those revisions and blames the line to its true substantive change. This is the standard remedy for whitespace or formatting-only churn.

followups.q: How does git blame -C differ from plain blame, and why does it matter?
followups.a: `-C` makes blame detect lines copied or moved from other files, attributing them to their original commit rather than the move. It is invaluable when code was refactored across files and plain blame would credit the move commit. The trade-off is it is slower, since Git searches more history.

followups.q: When would git log -S (pickaxe) beat both blame and log -L?
followups.a: When you want every commit where a specific string's occurrence count changed, for example to find when a function name was introduced and later removed anywhere in the repo. Blame shows only the current state of one file, and `-L` tracks one region. The pickaxe, especially `-G` for regex, scans the whole history for a token's lifecycle.

q: What are git hooks, and what is the difference between client-side and server-side hooks?
a: Hooks are scripts Git runs at lifecycle events. Client-side hooks like pre-commit and pre-push live in .git/hooks, run on the developer's machine, are not cloned, and can be skipped with --no-verify, so they are convenience gates, not guarantees. Server-side hooks like pre-receive and update run on the remote when a push lands and cannot be bypassed by the pusher, which is why real policy (protected branches, required checks) belongs there. Because .git/hooks is not versioned, teams share client hooks via a committed directory with core.hooksPath or a tool like Husky.
fs: Client hooks run locally, are not cloned, and --no-verify skips them; server hooks (pre-receive/update) run on the remote and enforce policy that cannot be bypassed.
fj: A pre-commit hook guarantees nobody can ever push bad code, so I rely on it as the only enforcement and never add server-side checks.

followups.q: What is the difference between pre-receive and update hooks on the server?
followups.a: `pre-receive` runs once for the entire push and sees all ref updates together, so it can accept or reject the push as a unit. `update` runs once per ref being updated, allowing per-branch decisions like protecting only `main`. Both run before refs change; choose based on whether your policy is whole-push or per-ref.

followups.q: Why might a CI pipeline be a better policy gate than a server pre-receive hook?
followups.a: Pre-receive hooks must run fast and synchronously, blocking the push, so they suit cheap checks like commit-message or branch-name rules. Full test suites and security scans belong in CI with required status checks, which run asynchronously and gate the merge rather than the push. Managed hosts like GitHub also do not expose custom server hooks, making CI the practical enforcement layer.

followups.q: How does a tool like Husky get around the fact that .git/hooks is not cloned?
followups.a: Husky installs hooks into a committed directory and points `core.hooksPath` there (via a prepare script on `npm install`), so every clone wires up the shared hooks automatically. This versions client hooks with the project. They remain bypassable with `--no-verify`, so they are still convenience, not enforcement.

q: What does git rerere do and when is it worth enabling?
a: rerere stands for reuse recorded resolution: when enabled, Git records how you resolved a merge conflict in .git/rr-cache, and the next time the exact same conflict appears it replays your resolution automatically. It pays off on long-lived branches and repeated rebases, where you would otherwise re-resolve the same conflicting hunks again and again, and it also speeds up rebasing a topic branch you keep replaying onto a moving main. You turn it on with git config --global rerere.enabled true; resolutions are local, so they are not shared across a team.
fs: rerere records conflict resolutions and auto-replays them on identical future conflicts, saving repeated rebases/merges on long-lived branches; it is local-only.
fj: There is no way to avoid re-resolving the same conflict; every rebase of a long-lived branch means hand-fixing the identical hunks from scratch.

followups.q: What is the danger of rerere silently replaying a resolution, and how do you stay safe?
followups.a: If your earlier resolution was wrong, rerere reapplies the same mistake automatically and may not flag the conflict, so the bug propagates quietly. Always review the result of an auto-resolved merge rather than trusting it blindly. You can clear a bad entry with `git rerere forget <path>` to force a fresh resolution.

followups.q: Since rerere is local-only, how can a team share resolutions of a recurring conflict?
followups.a: The `rr-cache` is not pushed, so each developer rebuilds it independently. To share, teams sometimes commit the `rr-cache` directory or, more robustly, resolve the conflict once on a shared integration branch so everyone rebases onto an already-merged base. Often the real fix is eliminating the long-lived divergence causing repeated conflicts.

followups.q: What exactly must match for rerere to recognize a conflict as the same one?
followups.a: rerere keys on the conflict's content, the pre-image of the conflicting hunks, not file names or commit SHAs. So the same textual conflict in a renamed file still matches, but any change to the surrounding conflicted lines makes it a new, unrecognized conflict. This content-based matching is why it works across repeated rebases of the same diverging change.

q: What is the difference between a git submodule and a subtree for vendoring another repo?
a: A submodule embeds a pointer to a specific commit of an external repository; the superproject records the URL and SHA, and the actual code is a separate clone you must init and update, which keeps the histories cleanly separate but adds steps everyone must remember. A subtree merges the external repo's contents directly into a subdirectory of your repo, so clones just work with no extra commands, at the cost of a larger history and a more involved update flow. Choose submodules when you want strict version isolation and shared ownership of the dependency; choose subtree when you prioritize a frictionless clone and contributor experience.
fs: Submodules pin an external repo by SHA as a separate clone needing init/update; subtree vendors the code into a subdir so clones just work but history grows.
fj: Submodules and subtrees are interchangeable; a fresh clone of a repo with submodules already contains all the submodule code with no extra commands.

code.label: submodule needs an extra init step

followups.q: Why does a submodule pointer go 'detached HEAD' inside the submodule, and what does that mean for updates?
followups.a: The superproject records a specific commit SHA, not a branch, so `submodule update` checks the submodule out at that exact commit in detached HEAD. To advance it you enter the submodule, checkout a branch, pull, then commit the new pointer in the superproject. Forgetting that two-step is why submodule updates trip people up.

followups.q: A common failure: a teammate pulled but the submodule code is missing or stale. What went wrong?
followups.a: They did not run `git submodule update --init --recursive` after pulling, so their superproject points at a new SHA the submodule directory has not checked out. The pointer moved but the nested clone did not follow. Setting `submodule.recurse = true` makes pulls update submodules automatically to avoid this.

followups.q: With subtree, how do you push local changes back to the upstream vendored repo?
followups.a: Use `git subtree push --prefix=libs/sdk <remote> <branch>`, which extracts the subdirectory's history into commits against the upstream. Subtree's strength is that contributors clone with nothing extra, but contributing back upstream is the awkward part, the inverse trade-off from submodules. Many teams vendor read-only with subtree and contribute upstream via a separate clone.

q: How do you use git reflog plus reset to rescue work after a catastrophic interactive rebase gone wrong?
a: When an interactive rebase finishes but the result is corrupt or wrong, the pre-rebase tip is recorded in reflog as ORIG_HEAD and in the branch's own reflog entries immediately before the rebase lines. Run `git reflog` to find the last good SHA, typically the entry just before the 'rebase (start)' line, then `git reset --hard <sha>` to snap the branch back there. Because reflog entries survive until gc, recovery is reliable if you act within the expiry window (90 days for reachable, 30 for unreachable). Never run `git gc --prune=now` until the branch is confirmed healthy.
fs: After a bad rebase, ORIG_HEAD and the branch reflog both point at the pre-rebase tip; reset --hard <sha> from reflog restores it before gc can prune the orphaned commits.
fj: If an interactive rebase finishes badly there is no recovery; I have to stash and redo everything because the old commits are gone once rebase completes.

followups.q: ORIG_HEAD is convenient, but when does it stop being reliable for rebase recovery?
followups.a: ORIG_HEAD is a single ref overwritten by the next destructive operation, such as a merge, reset, or another rebase. If you tried anything after the bad rebase before realizing it was wrong, ORIG_HEAD no longer points at the pre-rebase tip. In that case you must walk the reflog manually to find the correct pre-rebase entry, which is why reading the reflog directly is the more robust technique.

followups.q: You rebased interactively, then ran a merge that created a new ORIG_HEAD. How do you still recover the pre-rebase state?
followups.a: The branch's per-branch reflog (not HEAD's) still has every tip change in order. Run `git reflog <branchname>` to list them in reverse chronological order, find the entry immediately before the first rebase line, grab that SHA, and `git reset --hard` to it. The per-branch reflog is independent of ORIG_HEAD and persists the full movement history.

followups.q: How do you verify that the SHA you are about to reset to is actually the right one before committing to the reset?
followups.a: Run `git log <sha>` or `git show <sha>` to confirm the commit message and parent chain look as expected, then do a dry check with `git diff <sha> HEAD` to see what will change. Only then run `git reset --hard <sha>`. Creating a temporary branch (`git branch verify-recovery <sha>`) lets you inspect the restored state without moving your current branch, then you delete it or keep it.

q: Explain git bisect run with a flaky test and how you harden the bisect script against false verdicts.
a: When a test is intermittent, a single run may return the wrong exit code and mislabel a commit, causing bisect to converge on the wrong regression point. Hardening strategies: run the test N times in a loop and require all N to pass before returning 0 (good), so a single flaky pass does not whitewash a bad commit. Distinguish build failures (return 125 to skip) from test failures (return 1), since a build break should be skipped, not treated as the bug. Log output per commit to a file outside the repo so you can audit the verdicts after the fact. For persistent flakiness, consider running a deterministic proxy test that isolates the exact behaviour rather than the full test suite.
fs: Harden bisect-run against flaky tests by repeating N times (all must pass for good), returning 125 on build failure, and logging verdicts externally for post-run audit.
fj: For bisect run I just pass the test command directly; if the test is flaky I keep re-running bisect until I get the result I expect.

followups.q: Why does exit code 125 tell bisect to skip rather than marking the commit bad?
followups.a: Git reserves 125 as the 'untestable' signal for `bisect run`, so it skips the commit and picks a nearby one, equivalent to `git bisect skip`. Codes 128 and above abort the entire session. A build failure is not a test failure, so returning 1 there would mislabel a broken-build commit as the bug's introduction. Distinguishing these three categories keeps the binary search honest.

followups.q: After a bisect run finishes on a large repo, how do you confirm the identified commit is really the cause and not an artifact of the script?
followups.a: Manually check out the identified commit and the one before it, run the test multiple times on each, and compare results. Also inspect the commit's diff to see if the change is plausibly related to the symptom. If both passes and failures occur on both commits, the script is unreliable. It is also worth verifying the commit after the bad one does not pass, which would indicate an off-by-one in the bisect.

followups.q: In a monorepo where only certain services are affected, how do you limit bisect to relevant commits?
followups.a: Pass a pathspec after `--`: `git bisect start HEAD <good-sha> -- services/payments/`. Git skips commits that do not touch those paths, reducing the search space dramatically. Combine this with a fast service-specific test in the run script so each step tests only the affected component. This is the difference between bisecting through thousands of unrelated UI commits and narrowing to dozens of payment-service changes.

q: How do you use git submodule update --remote and what are its risks in a CI pipeline?
a: git submodule update --remote fetches the upstream branch tip of each submodule and checks it out, rather than the SHA pinned in the superproject. This is useful for update workflows where you deliberately want to advance a dependency, but in CI it breaks reproducibility: two runs at different times can check out different upstream commits and produce different builds without any change to the superproject's tree. The safe CI practice is to never use --remote in build pipelines; lock submodules to explicit SHAs in the superproject, run `git submodule update --init --recursive` to check out exactly those SHAs, and advance them deliberately via a dedicated update commit reviewed in a PR.
fs: --remote tracks the upstream branch tip for intentional advancement; CI must use locked SHA via `update --init --recursive` for reproducibility, never --remote.
fj: I always add --remote to git submodule update in CI so the build always gets the latest code from all dependencies automatically.

followups.q: A submodule's upstream repo was force-pushed, moving the branch tip. How does this affect your superproject?
followups.a: If your superproject still pins the old SHA, `git submodule update --init` will fail or produce an empty checkout because that commit no longer exists on the remote (or is unreachable). You must update the pinned SHA to a new reachable commit. This is why force-pushing a branch that submodule consumers track is a breaking change and should be avoided or coordinated carefully.

followups.q: How do you run a CI matrix that builds both the latest upstream version and the pinned version for regression comparison?
followups.a: Have two pipeline jobs: one using `git submodule update --init --recursive` for the pinned SHA, another that runs `git submodule update --remote` followed by the same build and tests. Fail the pipeline only on the locked job; treat the floating job as informational so a new upstream bug shows as a warning without blocking the release. Schedule the floating job on a cron, not every PR.

followups.q: What config option auto-updates submodules on every git pull without requiring an extra command?
followups.a: Set `submodule.recurse = true` (or `git config --global submodule.recurse true`). Git then appends `--recurse-submodules` to every pull, fetch, and checkout, keeping submodule working trees in sync automatically. The trade-off is longer pull times in large repos with many submodules, and you must still be deliberate about --remote vs locked-SHA semantics.

q: How does git partial clone (--filter=blob:none or --filter=tree:0) help teams working with large repositories?
a: Partial clone delays downloading objects until they are actually needed. `--filter=blob:none` (blobless clone) fetches all commits and trees upfront but omits file contents (blobs), downloading a blob only when you check it out or run a command that needs it. `--filter=tree:0` (treeless clone) goes further, omitting tree objects too, so only commits arrive initially. Both dramatically reduce clone time and disk for monorepos or repos with large binary histories. The trade-off is that some operations (like `git log --all -- path`) trigger on-demand fetches and need network access.
fs: blobless clone (--filter=blob:none) omits file content until checkout; treeless (--filter=tree:0) also omits trees; both slash clone time for large repos at the cost of lazy-fetch network hits.
fj: Partial clone is experimental and breaks git log and blame so it is not production-ready.

code.label: blobless and treeless clones
lines: # blobless: all commits+trees, blobs on demand

followups.q: What happens when a CI job clones blobless and then runs git log --all -- path/to/file?
followups.a: Git needs tree objects to resolve path history, so it issues on-demand fetches for any tree it does not have. On a busy CI machine this can hammer the server with many small requests, slowing the job and adding network load. Warm the cache with a targeted `git fetch --filter=blob:none origin` before path-based log commands, or use a blobless clone with a shallow depth to bound the on-demand fetch scope.

followups.q: How does Microsoft's Scalar tool go beyond partial clone to handle enormous repos like the Windows codebase?
followups.a: Scalar combines partial clone, sparse checkout (only materialise the directories you need), background fetch scheduling, GVFS protocol support, and a commit-graph file that makes `git log` and `git status` fast even with millions of commits. It also configures `core.fsmonitor` to avoid full working-tree scans. Partial clone is one layer; Scalar stacks multiple performance features to make a multi-million-file, multi-decade-history repo usable on a laptop.

followups.q: When should you prefer sparse checkout alongside partial clone versus a full blobless clone?
followups.a: Prefer sparse checkout (`git sparse-checkout set <patterns>`) when developers routinely work in one sub-area of a large monorepo and should never check out unrelated directories. Sparse checkout materializes only matched paths, reducing I/O and IDE indexing cost. A blobless clone without sparse checkout still materializes every file on checkout. Combining both gives maximum savings: sparse defines which paths, blobless defers the blobs of those paths until needed.

q: What is the commit-graph file and how does enabling it change git log and git status performance on large repos?
a: The commit-graph is a precomputed binary file at `.git/objects/info/commit-graph` that caches reachability, generation numbers, and parent relationships for all commits. Without it, commands like `git log`, `git merge-base`, and `git branch --merged` must walk the raw commit objects one by one, which is O(N) over history. With it, generation numbers enable fast reachability checks and topological sorts without traversing full chains. You write it with `git commit-graph write --reachable` and automate updates via `fetch.writeCommitGraph = true`; GitHub, GitLab, and Azure DevOps write it server-side automatically.
fs: commit-graph caches parent/reachability/generation data for all commits; enables O(log N) log and merge-base queries instead of O(N) object walks; write via fetch.writeCommitGraph=true.
fj: git log is always slow on large repos and there is nothing you can do about it except shallow clone.

followups.q: What is a generation number in the commit-graph, and why does it make merge-base lookups faster?
followups.a: Each commit gets a generation number equal to one plus the maximum generation of its parents, with root commits starting at 1. During a merge-base search, Git can prune entire subtrees: if one candidate has a higher generation than the other commit under investigation, it cannot be an ancestor without further traversal. This turns an exhaustive graph walk into a bounded search, which on repos with millions of commits can be orders of magnitude faster.

followups.q: After a force-push rewrites history, can the commit-graph become inconsistent, and how is it fixed?
followups.a: Yes. The graph may still cache the old commit chains, causing stale results for reachability queries until regenerated. Running `git commit-graph write --reachable --changed-paths` or a `git gc` regenerates it from the current object store. On server-side hosting this happens automatically after a force-push. On local clones, re-running `git fetch` with `fetch.writeCommitGraph = true` will write a fresh graph.

followups.q: How does enabling core.fsmonitor interact with the commit-graph to make git status fast on large working trees?
followups.a: `core.fsmonitor` delegates working-tree change detection to an OS file-system watcher (like Watchman), so Git receives only the actually modified paths rather than scanning every file. The commit-graph accelerates the commit-chain side of status (deciding what is staged vs committed). Together they address both the working-tree scan bottleneck and the history traversal bottleneck, which together explain why status is slow on a large, active repo.

q: How do you design and enforce git hooks as part of a team's development workflow, and what are the limits of hook-based enforcement?
a: Start by distinguishing client-side hooks (pre-commit, commit-msg, pre-push) from server-side hooks (pre-receive, update, post-receive). Client hooks catch issues early with fast, cheap checks: linting, unit tests, commit-message format. Share them via a committed `.githooks/` directory and `core.hooksPath`, installed automatically in a `prepare` npm/make script so every clone is wired up. However, client hooks can always be bypassed with `--no-verify`, so they are developer ergonomics, not security policy. True enforcement lives on the server: branch protection rules require green CI status checks before merge, block direct pushes to main, require PR approvals, and cannot be circumvented by client flags. Design the system so the client hooks give fast feedback and the server-side CI enforces the gate.
fs: Client hooks (pre-commit/commit-msg) give fast feedback via .githooks + core.hooksPath but are --no-verify bypassable; server branch protection + required CI status checks are the unbypassable gate.
fj: I added a pre-commit hook that runs the full test suite, so no bad code can ever merge, I do not need CI or branch protection rules.

code.label: wire up shared hooks + set CI gate
lines: # GitHub: require status check in branch protection

followups.q: A developer is blocked by a slow pre-commit hook that runs the full test suite. How do you fix the hook design?
followups.a: Pre-commit hooks should run in under a few seconds; move the full suite to CI. The hook should run only fast, targeted checks on the staged files: lint the changed files, run related unit tests via affected-test detection (e.g. Jest --findRelatedTests), or do a type-check. Full integration tests belong in a PR status check. Speed is why developers bypass hooks with --no-verify; making them fast removes the temptation.

followups.q: How do you enforce consistent commit-message format across an org without policing every developer manually?
followups.a: Add a `commit-msg` hook that validates the message against a pattern (Conventional Commits regex, for example) and exits 1 with a helpful error if it fails. Share it via `core.hooksPath`. On the server side, a `pre-receive` or CI job can re-validate the messages of all pushed commits, rejecting the push if any deviate. Tools like `commitlint` integrate with both layers and can be centrally configured in a shared config package.

followups.q: What is the difference between putting a check in a pre-push hook versus a pre-receive hook?
